grading.extracted_answer: To reconstruct the sequence of sibling-merge operations, perform a post-order traversal (bottom-up) on the final tree. For each node u in the final tree, identify its children v1, v2, ..., vk. These children are the results of merging groups of original siblings from the initial tree. 1. Maintain a mapping (e.g., an array or hash map) that tracks which original node IDs are represented by which node in the current final tree structure. 2. For each node u in the final tree, iterate through its children v1, v2, ..., vk in the order they appear in the final tree's adjacency list. 3. For each child vi, if vi represents a set of original siblings {s1, s2, ..., sm} that were merged, the merge operations are recorded as: - Merge s1 and s2 to get a new node s1,2. - Merge s1,2 and s3 to get s1,2,3, and so on, until sm is incorporated. 4. Because the problem requires a valid sequence, the post-order traversal ensures that all merges occurring within subtrees are completed before the merges at the current level are performed. This respects the dependency that a parent can only be merged after its constituent parts (the children) have been stabilized. 5. Output the sequence of merge operations (u, v) where u and v are the IDs of the nodes being merged at each step.
grading.grading_notes: The benchmark requires execution of hidden tests (binary_hidden_tests) to determine correctness, as it is a coding problem. The authoritative reference does not provide a solution to compare against, and the grading status must be 'ungraded' because the grader requires external execution which cannot be performed by the text-based grading agent.

Question ID: coding-B2-usaco-2023-us-open-gold-tree-merging
Title: B2. USACO 2023 US Open Gold / Tree Merging

Entry type: coding
Grader type hint: binary_hidden_tests

}

Problem:
Reconstruct a valid sequence of sibling-merge operations that transforms an initial rooted tree into a final rooted tree.

Binary hidden-test acceptance.

Submitted final result text:
FINAL_ANSWER: To reconstruct the sequence of sibling-merge operations, perform a post-order traversal (bottom-up) on the final tree. For each node u in the final tree, identify its children v1, v2, ..., vk. These children are the results of merging groups of original siblings from the initial tree. 1. Maintain a mapping (e.g., an array or hash map) that tracks which original node IDs are represented by which node in the current final tree structure. 2. For each node u in the final tree, iterate through its children v1, v2, ..., vk in the order they appear in the final tree's adjacency list. 3. For each child vi, if vi represents a set of original siblings {s1, s2, ..., sm} that were merged, the merge operations are recorded as: - Merge s1 and s2 to get a new node s1,2. - Merge s1,2 and s3 to get s1,2,3, and so on, until sm is incorporated. 4. Because the problem requires a valid sequence, the post-order traversal ensures that all merges occurring within subtrees are completed before the merges at the current level are performed. This respects the dependency that a parent can only be merged after its constituent parts (the children) have been stabilized. 5. Output the sequence of merge operations (u, v) where u and v are the IDs of the nodes being merged at each step. JUSTIFICATION: A post-order traversal ensures that we process the tree from the leaves up to the root. By resolving all merges within the subtrees of the children before merging the children themselves, we satisfy the structural constraints of the tree transformation, ensuring that every merge operation refers to nodes that currently exist in the evolving tree structure.

Judge whether the submitted answer should count as correct. Do not penalize harmless formatting or extra explanation if the actual answer is clearly right. If the answer is wrong or the benchmark is not gradeable from the reference above, say so.
